title: EV Support
intro: Range-aware routing for EVs — automatic charging stop planning, connector-matched station search, and battery threshold preferences.

overview.title: EV & Charging
overview.intro: End-to-end EV integration — from wiring the Battery Management System through charging station search, long-distance route planning, and the in-navigation driver UI.

arch.heading: How it all connects
arch.body: EV support in the UX Library spans five layers: the vehicle's BMS feeds live battery data upward through the Vehicle Integration API into the Navigation SDK. From there, the LDEVR API plans optimal charging stops, the EV Search API surfaces compatible stations, and the in-navigation UI keeps the driver informed throughout the journey.
arch.body2: Each layer is independently configurable — you can deploy charging search without LDEVR, or enable range-ring overlays without custom consumption models.

guide.heading: In this section
guide.body: Five pages cover the full integration journey. Start with Vehicle & Battery to establish accurate range predictions, then build outward.

battery.label: Vehicle & Battery
battery.desc: BMS setup, battery parameters, and consumption model with vehicle class presets.

charging_search.label: Charging Search
charging_search.desc: Connector-matched station discovery, real-time availability, and preferred network configuration.

routing.label: Long-Distance Routing
routing.desc: LDEVR API, automatic stop planning, and interactive threshold calculator.

nav_ui.label: In-Navigation UI
nav_ui.desc: SoC strip, reachable range ring, horizon stop countdown, and low-battery re-routing.

requirements.label: Requirements
requirements.desc: SDK dependencies, Android permissions, and integration checklist.

order.heading: Integration order
order.body: Follow this sequence to avoid blocked dependencies — the routing and search APIs depend on accurate vehicle data being set first.

s0.title: Add SDK dependencies
s0.desc: Add vehicle-integration-api, vehicle-provider, and search-ev to your build.gradle.

s1.title: Configure battery & consumption
s1.desc: Send accurate battery capacity, charge curve, and speed consumption curve via VehicleInfoManager.

s2.title: Initialise VehicleProvider
s2.desc: Set the initial SoC in the Navigation SDK and wire BMS callbacks for live updates at 1 Hz.

s3.title: Enable EV Search
s3.desc: Configure connector type filters and optionally set preferred charging networks (MSPs).

s4.title: Configure in-navigation UI
s4.desc: Enable the SoC strip, range ring, and horizon stop card. Set low-battery re-routing thresholds.

battery.title: Vehicle & Battery
battery.intro: Accurate vehicle parameters are the foundation of all EV features. The consumption model you set here determines the precision of every range prediction, charging stop, and reachable range calculation downstream.

presets.heading: Vehicle class presets
presets.body: Select a vehicle class to see representative battery parameters, charge rate and consumption curves, and the generated Kotlin configuration. These are starting-point values — use your vehicle's actual specification sheets for production.
presets.callout: These presets are illustrative. Always use your vehicle programme's validated consumption curves — deviating by more than 10% from actual values will cause measurable inaccuracy in charging stop placement.

aux.heading: Auxiliary power load
aux.body: Auxiliary power covers all non-drivetrain electrical load: climate control, infotainment, lighting, seat heating, and communication systems. In winter conditions, HVAC alone can consume 1–2 kW continuously, reducing effective range by 20–35%. Set the auxiliaryPower parameter to the 5-minute rolling average your vehicle programme measures.
aux.callout: Auxiliary power is the single most under-specified parameter in EV integrations. A 500 W default is used when not supplied — for cold-climate or SUV deployments this can be 3–4× too low, leading to charging stops appearing later than they should.

offset.heading: Charging time offset
offset.body: The chargingTimeOffset accounts for the time spent physically connecting and disconnecting the charge cable — typically 60–180 seconds. The default is 60 s. For premium vehicles with automated charging arms, or for fleet deployments where cable handling time is documented, adjust this value to improve total journey time accuracy in route planning.

chargingSearch.title: Charging Search
chargingSearch.intro: The EV Search API surfaces compatible charging stations nearby, along the route, or near the destination. Connector type matching, real-time availability, and preferred network prioritisation are all configurable.

msp.heading: Preferred charging networks
msp.body: OEMs can configure a prioritised list of Mobility Service Providers (MSPs). The LDEVR API uses this list to prefer stops at those networks when stop quality is otherwise equal. Select and reorder the networks relevant to your deployment region.
msp.empty: No networks selected — LDEVR will choose stops by optimal time only.
msp.callout: MSP preferences influence stop selection when multiple stations offer equivalent quality. They do not override the minimum SoC thresholds or force sub-optimal routing to reach a preferred network.

routing.title: Long-Distance Routing
routing.intro: The LDEVR API calculates the optimal sequence of charging stops for routes beyond single-charge range. Adjust the minimum SoC thresholds below to see how they affect stop count, total charge time, and journey duration.

strategy.heading: Charging stop strategy
strategy.body: Drag the sliders to adjust minimum SoC thresholds. The route timeline updates in real time — note the trade-off between safety buffer and total journey time.
strategy.callout: Lower thresholds reduce stop count and journey time but leave less buffer for unexpected consumption. TomTom recommends 15% at stops and destination as a baseline for most markets.

navUI.title: In-Navigation UI
navUI.intro: The in-navigation EV UI keeps the driver informed throughout the journey — state of charge, estimated range, next charging stop, and automatic re-routing when battery drops below threshold.

soc.heading: State of charge strip
soc.body: The SoC strip appears below the NIP during active navigation. It shows current battery percentage, estimated range remaining, and — when SoC drops below the low threshold — the next planned charging stop. Select a battery state below to preview how the UI adapts.

rangeRing.heading: Reachable range overlay
rangeRing.body: The reachable range ring (isoline) visualises how far the vehicle can travel on current charge. Charging stations within the ring are highlighted in green; those outside are dimmed. The ring updates every 30 seconds during navigation.
rangeRing.hint: Select a SoC state above to see how the ring shrinks as battery depletes.
rangeRing.callout: The range ring is computed by the TomTom Reachable Range API. It accounts for the current consumption model, remaining SoC, and real-time traffic — not a simple radius.

reroute.heading: Low-battery re-routing
reroute.body: When SoC drops below the configured threshold during navigation, the SDK automatically recalculates the route to include the nearest suitable charging stop.
reroute.b1: Next charging stop added to horizon panel. Driver notified.
reroute.b2: Route immediately recalculated to nearest charger. Warning banner shown.
reroute.b3: Route recalculated with closer stop. Previous stop removed from plan.
reroute.t4: Driver skips a planned charging stop
reroute.b4: Route recalculated from current position. SoC at destination re-evaluated.
reroute.callout: Re-routing to a charging stop overrides active detour avoidances — the SDK will route through toll roads or unpaved segments if they are required to reach a charger safely.

requirements.title: Requirements
requirements.intro: SDK dependencies, Android permissions, and a step-by-step integration checklist — everything needed before going live with EV features.
requirements.heading: SDK dependencies

checklist.heading: Integration checklist
checklist.body: Work through each phase in order. Later phases depend on earlier ones being complete — particularly vehicle data accuracy affecting routing quality.
checklist.complete: All steps complete — your EV integration is ready for testing.

items.depVehicle: Add vehicle-integration-api and vehicle-provider to build.gradle
items.permissions: Declare INTERNET, ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION, and VEHICLE_INTEGRATION permissions
items.bmsConnect: Connect VehicleInfoManager to OEM BMS callback at 1 Hz
items.batteryParams: Send currentBatteryCapacity and stateOfEnergy via sendBatteryInfo()
items.connectors: Send vehicleConnectors matching the vehicle's physical connector types
items.vehicleProvider: Set Vehicle.Car with ElectricEngine in VehicleProvider at app start
items.socInit: Initialise ChargeLevel with current SoC before starting navigation
items.socUpdate: Wire addVehicleUpdatedListener to update SoC from BMS in real time
items.evSearch: Configure EvSearchOptions with connector filter and geoBias
items.msp: Set preferredMSPs list in LDEVR request if commercial MSP agreements apply
items.ldevr: Verify LDEVR route returns charging stops on a route exceeding vehicle range
items.socStrip: Enable EVNavigationConfiguration with showStateOfCharge and showRemainingRange
items.rangeRing: Enable ReachableRangeConfiguration and verify ring updates during navigation
items.horizonStop: Confirm charging stop card appears in Horizon Panel below low-battery threshold
items.reroute: Test low-battery re-routing by simulating SoC drop below critical threshold

permissions.heading: Android permissions
permissions.body: Declare all four permissions in AndroidManifest.xml. ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION and VEHICLE_INTEGRATION require runtime permission requests on Android 6+.
permissions.callout: Missing VEHICLE_INTEGRATION permission causes VehicleInfoManager to silently fail — BMS data will not reach the SDK and range calculations will fall back to defaults.

vehicleSetup.heading: Vehicle setup
vehicleSetup.body: Before EV features activate, the SDK needs an accurate model of the vehicle's battery, energy consumption, efficiency, and supported connector types. These parameters feed directly into route range calculations — incorrect values will produce inaccurate charging stop predictions.

vehicleApi.heading: Vehicle Integration API
vehicleApi.body: Use VehicleInfoManager (from the Vehicle Integration Library) to send battery state and consumption data. Parameters should be updated whenever the BMS reports a change — the SoC update frequency should be at 1 Hz and fluctuation kept below 0.3%.
vehicleApi.callout: Consumption curves must cover a speed range from 10 km/h to maximum vehicle speed with 15–25 definition points. Values are baseline at 20°C — auxiliary power defaults to 500 W (climate, infotainment, lighting) if not supplied.

vehicleProvider.heading: Navigation SDK — VehicleProvider
vehicleProvider.body: The Navigation SDK maintains its own vehicle state via VehicleProvider. Set the initial charge level at app start and keep it updated reactively using BMS callbacks.

params.currentBatteryCapacity: Current usable battery capacity. Range: [0, nominal].
params.stateOfEnergy: Current charge. Update at 1 Hz; fluctuation < 0.3%.
params.speedConsumptionCurve: 15–25 points. 10 km/h → max speed. Baseline at 20°C.
params.vehicleConnectors: Drives station compatibility filtering in search.

evSearch.heading: EV charging search
evSearch.body: The EV charging search panel lets drivers find compatible stations nearby, along the route, or near their destination. Filters include charging speed tiers, preferred operators, charging cards, and nearby services. Results show real-time availability and connector compatibility based on the vehicle profile.

searchOptions.heading: Search options
searchOptions.body: Use EvSearchOptions to configure the search. When vehicleConnectors is set in the vehicle profile, the SDK automatically filters results by connector compatibility — stations are marked as compatible or incompatible in the detail view.

realtimeDetails.heading: Real-time station details
realtimeDetails.body: After an initial search, retrieve live connector availability and payment details by Place ID. This is useful to refresh data when the driver taps a station or when the route recalculates.

options.connectors: Connector type filter. Automatically populated from vehicle profile.
options.status: Status.Available filters to open connectors only.
options.nearbyPoiCategories: Request nearby amenities: Restaurant, HotelMotel, CafePub, PublicAmenity, Shop, ParkRecreationArea.

ldevr.heading: Long distance EV routing
ldevr.body: For journeys beyond the vehicle's single-charge range, the LDEVR API automatically calculates the optimal sequence of charging stops. The driver sees a route timeline with each stop's arrival SoC, target SoC, charging time, and operator. Charging is re-evaluated dynamically if battery consumption deviates from prediction.
ldevr.callout1: The Plan charging automatically toggle is visible to the driver in the route preview panel. When disabled, the route is shown as-is — if it's out of range a warning banner appears. The default state (on/off) can be set at the SDK configuration level.
ldevr.callout2: The traffic parameter must be set to true — the LDEVR endpoint requires live traffic data and will reject requests with it disabled.

apiRequest.heading: LDEVR API request
apiRequest.body: The route calculation is a POST request to the LDEVR endpoint. Vehicle parameters passed via the Vehicle Integration API are automatically forwarded by the Navigation SDK — you only need to call the REST endpoint directly for custom integrations or server-side planning.

fields.remainingChargeAtArrivalInkWh: Battery state when arriving at this stop or destination.
fields.targetChargeInkWh: Target charge level after charging at this stop.
fields.totalChargingTimeInSeconds: Total charging time across all stops.

preferences.heading: Route preferences
preferences.body: The route preferences panel is accessible from the route preview screen. Drivers can set minimum battery thresholds at charging stops and at the destination, and opt to avoid toll roads or unpaved roads. These preferences map directly to LDEVR API parameters.
preferences.callout: While driving, actual arrival SoC may drop up to 2% below the configured minimum due to real-time consumption variance — this is expected behaviour shown in the UI disclaimer.
